text: The patient is positioned left side up on their flank. The surgeon places the left hand operating port subcostally in the midclavicular line, while the right hand operating port is positioned three finger breadths supramedial to the anterior superior iliac spine. The camera port is triangulated between these two ports using a Hassan cut-down technique.

Depending upon the body mass index of the patient, the position of the camera port will be variable and the umbilicus is not used as a landmark unless in the very slim patient an umbilical camera port is required.

The surgeon observes the benign non-functioning kidney with multiple large cysts prolapsing through the mesentery medial to the descending colon. An initial superficial incision is made in the posterior peritoneum, carefully dividing only the peritoneum without penetrating the fascial layer covering the durotus fat.

The surgeon mobilizes the colon, reflecting it medially along with the mesocolic fat to create space anterior to the kidney. The mesocolic fat is carefully brought forward with the colon, avoiding dissection through it to maintain anatomical orientation and minimize bleeding. The Girota's fascia is left intact as the plane is developed, marking an important initial step in the left-sided nephrectomy.

The surgeon carefully develops the dissection plane over Girota's fat, avoiding trauma to the fascia. If holes are created in the fascia, adjustments are made to continue dissection at a different site. The presence of inflammatory conditions or previous bleeding in the kidney can complicate this process.

The surgeon uses the left hand to retract the mesocolon superiorly and laterally, revealing the gonadal vein and ureter adjacent to the lower pole of the left kidney. Once the ureter is identified, the surgeon places the left hand beneath it to lift it laterally, facilitating the division between locking clips.

The surgeon divides the gonadal vein, which is positioned lateral to the ureter, using locking clips. The dissection continues seamlessly without the use of a harmonic energy device. Following the division of the vein, the surgeon proceeds with dissection on the lateral aspect of the kidney to detach it from the abdominal wall.
The surgeon divides the ureter, completing its dissection and leaving it unattended as the focus shifts away from the renal hilum to avoid lateral dissection that could compromise safety.
The surgeon reflects the colon medially off the anterior surface of the kidney, encountering difficulty due to the enlarged kidney caused by large, previously complicated renal cysts.

The surgeon uses a left hand retractor to apply tension and carefully lift the posterior surface of the colon off the anterior surface of the kidney. As the dissection progresses towards the splenic flexure, the surgeon checks both sides to avoid inadvertently approaching the splenic flexure with the energy device.

The surgeon reflects the posterior surface of the colon off the entire length of the kidney, addressing adhesions between the peritoneum and the fascia overlaying Girota's fat. The lower pole of the kidney is lifted laterally, facilitating exploration of the plane leading to the renal hilum. Additional adhesions on the posterior surface of the colon are carefully managed.

The surgeon uses the left hand as a strong retractor to apply tension to the tissue layers, facilitating the division of fascia overlying the lower pole of the kidney. This maneuver allows further lateral reflection and exposes the inferior margin of the renal hilum. Three ports are visible: one for the camera and two for operating instruments.

The surgeon continues dissection medial to the course of the ureter, ignoring it as a landmark. The kidney is lifted more laterally, while the colon is reflected medially, exposing the surgical plane.

The surgeon identifies pulsation within the mesocolon at the seven o'clock position in the image. Further mobilization of the colon is performed to gain clear access to the renal hilum, separating adhesions between the posterior surface of the splenic flexure and the superior margin of the kidney. Intermittent checks ensure the spleen is not inadvertently approached during dissection.

The surgeon applies traction and counter traction to atraumatically separate tissue planes, using an energy device to manage any adhesions. The posterior surface of the splenic flexure of the colon is positioned behind the left hand retractor, while the spleen is located just behind the fascial layer being cleaned.

The surgeon divides adhesions overlaying the renal hilum, revealing the thin fascial layer overlying Girota's fat at the base of the image. This fascia is carefully divided to access the renal hilar vessels.

The surgeon encounters the gonadal vein, visible from the one o'clock to seven o'clock position. Using a harmonic scalpel, the surgeon exposes and divides the thin fascial layer, sweeping away the fat to reveal the renal vein.

The surgeon performs suction dissection parallel to the anterior surface of the left renal vein, carefully avoiding disruption of accessory veins. The harmonic scalpel moves in alignment with the vein's direction to minimize risk to delicate venous structures. The inferior edge of the left renal vein becomes visible during this dissection.

The surgeon carefully divides the fascia overlying the renal vein, ensuring visibility through the tissue to avoid damaging the lumbar vein. This exposes the insertion of the lumbar vein into the inferior margin of the renal vein.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to point to and divide the fascia overlying the lumbar vein, carefully identifying the triangle between the medial aspect of the lumbar vein and the inferior margin of the left renal vein. This division is crucial for accessing the renal artery without complications.
The surgeon uses irrigation to gently free adherent fascia from the surface of the renal vein, gradually exposing the tributaries of the vein without causing trauma.

The surgeon lifts the renal vein anteriorly to access the renal artery, avoiding the ureter and gonadal vein paths for a simpler dissection. The harmonic scalpel opens the triangle between the lumbar vein and renal vein, facilitating the division of the lumbar vein.
The surgeon applies constant tension with the left hand to safely lift fascia away from the vascular structures while using the harmonic scalpel to apply heat energy during the dissection.

You'll note that during this whole dissection, the camera has remained within the patient and has not been removed for cleaning. If the camera becomes splattered with fat or blood, then cleaning with the suction irrigation whilst the camera remains in situ is more than adequate. Constant removal and reinsertion of the camera for cleaning delays the procedure, and if it's done during a time of active bleeding, can in fact lead to harm. by delaying the control of any bleeding.

The renal vein is visible, oriented vertically from twelve to six o'clock, with the lumbar vein branching off laterally. The pulsating aorta is observed running diagonally from a five o'clock to eleven o'clock position, encircled by the renal and lumbar veins.

The surgeon carefully cleans the lumbar vein and divides it between locking clips, ensuring the clip is positioned to minimize perivascular adhesions. Once the lumbar vein is divided, the renal artery becomes visible, appearing as a single vessel that has not yet branched.
The surgeon approaches the renal artery as it emerges from the wall of the aorta, facilitating a radical dissection that maximizes the length of the renal artery for transplantation purposes.

The renal artery is visible lateral to the renal vein. The surgeon uses a right-angle instrument to create a space, ensuring the pointed tips are directed away from the renal vein. After creating the space, the artery is divided using a linear stapler, which places three rows of staples for secure closure.

The surgeon approaches the renal artery from the right-hand port, aligning the application of clips parallel to the aorta. Additional locking clips are placed medial to the staple line on the patient side and lateral on the specimen side, ensuring secure division of the artery.

The surgeon prepares to divide the renal vein, having created a space above it for clip application. The adrenal vein is visible entering the superior margin of the renal vein. A row of staples is applied to the renal vein before additional locking clips are placed.

The adrenal gland becomes visible at the inferior margin of the dissection. The surgeon continues the dissection line superiorly, lateral to the adrenal gland, unless performing a radical nephrectomy that includes the adrenal gland, in which case the adrenal vein would be divided and the gland included with the specimen.

The harmonic scalpel divides tissue just lateral to the visible adrenal vein. The hilum, lateral, and some anterior attachments of the kidney have been divided. The kidney remains attached by adhesions in the superior medial groove, which can be divided from above or below. The pancreas and splenic vein are visible beyond the scalpel tip, necessitating careful lateral retraction to maintain tension on adhesions and ensure a clear dissection path.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection away from the kidney, incorporating Gerota's fascia and surrounding fat into the specimen, creating a less vascular and oncologically sound plane.

Whilst the dissection continues both medially and laterally, I'll point out some of the key factors in our enhanced recovery programme. We don't routinely place a renal drain. We don't routinely place a urethral catheter. All patients are counselled about this in anticipation of their surgery and are consented for the approximately ten percent chance of requiring a single episode of intermittent self-catheterisation post-op. Patients are mobilised on the evening of surgery and they're given free access to whatever diet and fluid they choose after the operation. The chewing of gum in the post-operative day can reduce the risk of post-operative ileus if the patient has not returned to full diet. We aim for discharge the day following surgery.

The surgeon divides the final adhesions at the upper pole of the kidney, using traction to expose the plane of division. The dissection is performed close to the kidney, with the tail of the pancreas visible behind the fascia to the left.

The surgeon inspects the kidney, which is almost free, and divides the final adhesion at the upper pole, freeing the specimen. Bleeding points are controlled using an energy device, with clips applied if necessary, especially near the adrenal gland. The hilum is inspected for bleeding. The kidney is placed back in the renal bed, and an endoscopic bag is introduced to scoop the kidney for delivery through the left iliac fossa port. A final inspection for bleeding is conducted before wound closure, completing the operation.
